elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate the impact of fuzzy dark matter (FDM) on supermassive black holes (SMBHs) characterized by a spherical charge distribution.
elements.sent-2411-002: This work introduces a new class of spherically symmetric, self-gravitational relativistic charged models for FDM haloes, using the Einasto density model.
elements.sent-2411-003: This study enables the dark matter (DM) to appear as the matter ingredient, which constructs the black hole and extends the non-commutative mini black hole stellar solutions.
elements.sent-2411-004: By considering the charged anisotropic energy-momentum tensor with an equation of state (EoS) $p_{r =-\rho$, we explore various black hole solutions for different values of the Einasto index and mass parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Our approach suggests that the central density of the resulting black hole model mimics the usual de Sitter core.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, we discuss the possibility of constructing a charged self-gravitational droplet by replacing the above-mentioned EoS with a non-local one.
elements.sent-2411-007: However, under these circumstances, the radial pressure is observed to be negative.
elements.sent-2411-008: Ultimately, we consider various possibilities of constructing DM black holes, featuring intermediate masses that could evolve into galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-009: Consequently, some of these theoretical models have the potential to replace the usual black hole solutions of the galactic core.
elements.sent-2411-010: Simultaneously, these models are physically beneficial for being comprised of the fundamental matter component of the cosmos.
elements.sent-2411-011: Due to the outcomes of this paper, we would be able to study the connection between BH and DM by formulating stable stellar structures featuring fuzzy mass distributions derived from the Einasto distribution of DM halos.
elements.sent-2411-012: The relationship between astrophysical dark entities, such as black holes (BHs) and dark matter (DM), presents a fascinating and complex challenge in modern cosmology and astrophysics.
elements.sent-2411-013: Compact objects like BHs are cosmic vacuums where the gravitational field is highly dominant, opening new avenues for understanding the interaction between gravity and fundamental physics \cite{brown2016complexity,alishahiha2019black,yousaf2024role .}
elements.sent-2411-014: They originate from the collapse of massive stars or the merging of dense objects and have been thoroughly studied using Einstein's gravitational model.
elements.sent-2411-015: On the other hand, DM makes up about $27\%$ of the total mass energy of the universe and remains an intriguing puzzle for researchers \cite{arkani2009theory,yousaf2024imprints .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Despite its pivotal influence in shaping large-scale cosmic structures, DM has not yet been directly observed.
elements.sent-2411-017: Different models have been suggested to explain the nature of DM, with the fuzzy dark matter (FDM) model emerging as a strong contender.
elements.sent-2411-018: The FDM model has attracted the attention of researchers due to its unique characteristics and implications for cosmic structures.
elements.sent-2411-019: The FDM model is defined by its wave-like features, which stem from the quantum mechanical nature of light bosons with masses around $10^{-22}$ eV, including axions and other similar particles.
elements.sent-2411-020: The wave-like nature of FDM distinguishes it from other DM candidates, like cold DM, and has significant consequences for the structure and development of self-gravitating cosmic objects.
elements.sent-2411-021: Unique cosmic phenomena, such as interference patterns in density distributions, smooth cores in dark matter halos, and inhibited structure formation at small scales, are observed in FDM at both galactic and cosmic scales.
elements.sent-2411-022: These unique properties of FDM offer a fresh perspective on DM, particularly in regions of strong gravitational fields, like those near BHs.
elements.sent-2411-023: The motivation for exploring FDM in the context of BHs stems from the crucial role that DM, in any of its forms, plays in the dynamics of self-gravitational stellar systems.
elements.sent-2411-024: The existence of DM in close vicinity to BHs can affect their stability, evolution, and observable characteristics, including emission spectra, accretion processes, and gravitational wave signals.
elements.sent-2411-025: The distinctive quantum features associated with FDM further intensify its interaction with BHs, especially in regions of high density and intense curvature.
elements.sent-2411-026: The dynamics of matter and energy surrounding BHs can be affected by electromagnetic fields, which introduces another dimension to the study of BHs \cite{jimenez2021charged .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Electromagnetic radiation can interact with BHs, especially those that are charged or surrounded by magnetic fields \cite{vogelsberger2012subhaloes,zavala2013constraining,vogelsberger2013direct .}
elements.sent-2411-028: A wide range of phenomena are produced by this interaction, including the production of relativistic jets, dynamics of the accretion disk, and energy extraction through the Penrose process.
elements.sent-2411-029: Under these conditions, FDM may alter the structure of the electromagnetic field and affect its behavior in ways that classical DM theories do not foresee \cite{ansoldi2007non,nicolini2006noncommutative .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Studying this interaction is vital for understanding the observation data from BH systems.
elements.sent-2411-031: This data includes X-ray emissions, radio signals, and the recently detected gravitational wave signals resulting from BH mergers.
elements.sent-2411-032: The existence of ultra-light bosonic particles in the FDM model produce wave-like characteristics on galactic scales.
elements.sent-2411-033: The ``cusp-core'' and ``missing satellite'' issues that are frequently encountered in cold DM simulations could be resolved by the FDM model by naturally smoothing out small-scale density fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-034: Although observations have limited the FDM model, it remains a viable possibility.
elements.sent-2411-035: Certain astronomical data, such as the matter distribution in galactic halos and the rotation curves of dwarf galaxies, indicate that FDM might still be a good fit in some regimes.
elements.sent-2411-036: Based on the particular model under consideration, the limitations of the FDM model could alter when more accurate data become available.
elements.sent-2411-037: Other alternatives, such as mixed DM models or more complex FDM interactions, are still being explored, even if existing observations may rule out some parameter ranges (such as certain masses of FDM particles).
elements.sent-2411-038: There is still much to learn about the whole effect of FDM on the creation of stellar structures.
elements.sent-2411-039: Investigating the impact of ultralight scalar fields on gravitationally bound stellar structures could open up new directions in fundamental physics, including possibly relating DM to inflationary models in the early universe or string theory.
elements.sent-2411-040: To acquire a better knowledge of the nature of BH, it is essential to explore their interior composition in more detail.

elements.sent-2411-042: Furthermore, understanding the type of matter that contributed to the BH's formation is equally essential for a comprehensive understanding \cite{bambi2013non,malafarina2015compact .}
